### Table of Contents

## Resources for social media data

Social media data come from various resources, such as Facebook, Bluesky, Mastodon, Reddit, Instagram or YouTube. The elements of social media data may be:

* individual tweets, comments on Facebook, Bluesky, X, Mastodon or Reddit etc.,
* visual content, such as photos or videos,
* network connections between network users (friend connections, groups),
* data on ratings and/or interests (preferences or likes).

Social media data are available to researchers, but their availability is restricted by companies that own respective social media platforms (Facebook, X, Bluesky, Mastodon, etc.). Restricted availability of social media data represents serious obstacle for more intensive application of social media data in social research.

There are several reasons for the limited availability of social media data. One of them is legal and deals with the social media content’s copyright. The users have copyright for their own content (e.g. tweets or posts) and by signing terms of use they give the social media platform a license to use the content for various purposes. The use of the social media data for third parties (private companies, academic researchers etc.) is restricted in the terms of use. This constrains the researchers (and data archives) in using, storing and sharing the data. A good source of guidance on social media data preservation both for researchers and repositories is Thomson, S.D. (2016) ”[Preserving Social Media](https://www.dpconline.org/docs/technology-watch-reports/1486-twr16-01/file)”.

One of other reasons for the limited availability of social media data lies in the ethics. Researchers and data archivist must care about the protection of personal information of the social media users.

##### [Platforms as social media data sources](#collapse4968)

Social media data can be obtained through the application programming interfaces (APIs) of the social media platforms. However, these APIs usually restrict the type and amount of data you can collect. If researchers request large amounts of data through APIs, they might not get the complete data but samples. Often it is not fully transparent how these data are sampled.

For those who are not able to handle APIs for downloading the data, there are commercial subjects that sell social media data, such as [Gnip](https://docs.x.com/x-api/enterprise-gnip-2.0/fundamentals/overview) (acquired by Twitter Inc. in 2014) or [DataSift](https://catalog.docnow.io/), but these usually have high costs.

##### [Social Media Data in European Data Archives](#collapse4969)

According to the results of a survey carried out among European social science data archives for [SERISS project](https://seriss.eu) in June 2019, only two CESSDA archives store and disseminate social media data so far: GESIS and UK Data Service (UKDS) offer their users limited collection of social media data, Facebook data, geo-coded Twitter data, and specific subsets of Wikipedia. In particular, UKDS holds several Twitter data sets (20 collections of Twitter communication (tweets’ IDs, timestamp, hashtags).

Currently, several CESSDA archives plan strategies to overcome legal and technical issues related to social media data archiving and sharing as they see it as important area.

##### [General repositories](#collapse4970)

[Zenodo](https://zenodo.org/), [Harvard Dataverse](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/) or [Figshare](https://figshare.com/) hold limited but increasing number of social media datasets. These repositories obtain data through self-archiving i.e. without archive taking care over data and metadata quality.

##### [Field specific and thematic social media data sources](#collapse4971)

There exist several projects and institutions that ingest and store social media data on various topics. Some of them are:

* The [CrisisLex](https://crisislex.org/) is a repository of crisis-related social media data and tools.
* The Schlesinger Library on the History of Women in America has created dataset “[#metoo Digital Media Collection](https://www.schlesinger-metooproject-radcliffe.org/)”.
* [Stanford Network Analysis Project](http://snap.stanford.edu/) is a repository for data from internet-based social networks.
* T[weetsKB](http://l3s.de/tweetsKB/): A Public and Large-Scale RDF Corpus of Annotated Tweets
* The [Documenting the Now](https://www.docnow.io/catalog/) catalog of Twitter data collections
* The [TweetSets](https://tweetsets.library.gwu.edu/) collection of Twitter data sets

Social media data can be linked to survey data to expand research opportunities. Watch this video to discover more: Johannes Breuer discusses what data linking is and why it is useful to link survey and social media data. If you would like to access the full PowerPoint presentation upon which this video is based, you can access it on [Zenodo](http://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.3923901).

Please cite this video as: Breuer, Johannes. (2020, June). Data Linking I: Survey Data & Social Media Data. Presented at the CESSDA Training Days 2019 (CTD2019), Cologne, Germany: Zenodo. <http://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.3923901>

--- SOURCE 4: Big Data Analytics in IoT, social media, NLP, and information security ---
URL: https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9

SUMMARY:
This paper presents a comprehensive, domain-specific survey and experimental evaluation of machine learning techniques for Big Data Analytics across four critical domains: IoT, Social Media, Natural Language Processing (NLP), and Information Security. Despite the increasing number of survey papers in Big Data Analytics across domains such as IoT, Social Media, NLP, and Information Security, many fail to provide a structured and application-specific categorization of the machine learning techniques employed. This section presents a novel taxonomy that classifies machine learning techniques in Big Data Analytics based on their functional roles across IoT, Social Media, NLP, and Information Security. A novel taxonomic framework that systematically classifies machine learning algorithms used in Big Data Analytics across key domains—IoT, Social Media, NLP, and Information Security—based on their specific application areas and functional characteristics.

FULL CONTENT:

Big Data Analytics in IoT, social media, NLP, and information security: trends, challenges, and applications
============================================================================================================

*   [Kamal Taha](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#auth-Kamal-Taha-Aff1)[1](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#Aff1)

[_Journal of Big Data_](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/)**volume 12**, Article number:150 (2025) [Cite this article](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#citeas)

*   440 Accesses

*   [Metrics details](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9/metrics)

Abstract
--------

This paper presents a comprehensive, domain-specific survey and experimental evaluation of machine learning techniques for Big Data Analytics across four critical domains: IoT, Social Media, Natural Language Processing (NLP), and Information Security. A novel taxonomic framework is introduced to classify and analyze the suitability of algorithms based on empirical, experimental, and computational perspectives. The study integrates large-scale experimental benchmarking of key techniques—including CNN, XGBoost, Self-Supervised Learning (SSL), Graph Neural Networks (GNN), ELM, KNN, and Decision Trees—using real-world and synthetic datasets, and evaluates them across five performance metrics: accuracy, F1-score, precision, recall, and computational time. Key findings reveal that: (1) GNN and Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) are top performers in terms of predictive performance and efficiency in domains such as IoT and Social Media, (2) XGBoost and CNN offer superior accuracy and robustness across structured and unstructured data tasks, though CNN incurs higher computational costs, (3) ELM and Decision Trees are better suited for lightweight or interpretable applications, and (4) KNN generally underperforms in scalability and predictive strength for large-scale tasks. The taxonomy and experiments collectively demonstrate the need for context-aware algorithm selection, particularly for real-time and scalable Big Data applications. By aligning algorithmic properties with domain-specific challenges, this study offers actionable insights for researchers and practitioners seeking effective analytic strategies in the evolving landscape of Big Data.

Introduction
------------

Big data, fueled by the influx of information from sources like the internet, cloud platforms, and biosensors, is revolutionizing the modern information landscape [[1](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR1 "Huang Z, Destech Publicat I. Research on the innovation of E-business talents training mode under the background of big data. Intern conference on E-commerce and contemporary economic development. DEStech Transactions on Economics Business and Management, 2018. p. 48–52."), [2](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR2 "Ikegwu AC, Nweke HF, Anikwe CV, et al. Big Data Analytics for data-driven industry: a review of data sources, tools, challenges, solutions, and research directions. Cluster Comput. 2022;25:3343–87.")]. Global initiatives are maximizing its strategic potential. Companies like Wal-Mart and Facebook, for instance, regularly process petabytes of data [[3](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR3 "Sagiroglu S, Sinanc D. Big data: a review. presented in international conference: collaboration technologies and systems (CTS). IEEE Xplore, 2013.")]. However, the challenge goes beyond storage to the extraction of meaningful insights. Technologies, such as Apache Spark built on Hadoop, are emerging to cater to the escalating data demands [[4](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR4 "Narayanan V, Vijayakumar VB, Venkatraman SR, Baruah PK. Semantic node embeddings of distributed graphs using apache spark. 2016 Fourth International Conference on Parallel, Distributed and Grid Computing (PDGC), Waknaghat, India, 2016; pp. 709–713."), [5](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR5 "El-kenawy ESM, Khodadadi N, Mirjalili S, Abdelhamid AA, Eid MM, Ibrahim A. Greylag goose optimization: nature-inspired optimization algorithm. Expert Syst Appl. 2024;238:122147.")]. Eric Topol emphasized how the future of medicine will see AI intertwined with human intellect [[6](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR6 "Ramkumar PN, Haeberle HS, Patterson BM, Krebs VE. Artificial intelligence and arthroplasty at a single institution: real-world applications of machine learning to big data, value-based care, mobile health, and remote patient monitoring. J Arthroplasty. 2019;34(10):2204–9.")]. In the healthcare sector, especially in the U.S., the priority is on managing massive medical data sets while simultaneously confronting economic hurdles.

Artificial intelligence (AI), boosted by technological progress and rapid processing, has evolved from a concept to a functional tool [[7](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR7 "Mathew DE, Ebem DU, Ikegwu AC, et al. Recent emerging techniques in explainable artificial intelligence to enhance the interpretable and understanding of AI models for human. Neural Process Lett. 2025;57:16.")]. Machine learning (ML), a subset of AI, emphasizes automating data analysis to identify patterns in large datasets [[8](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR8 "Mahadevkar S, et al. A review on machine learning styles in computer vision—techniques and future directions. IEEE Access. 2022;10:107293–329."), [9](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR9 "Khafaga DS, Alhussan AA, El-kenawy ESM, Ibrahim A, Elkhalik SHA, El-Mashad SY, Abdelhamid AA. Improved prediction of metamaterial antenna bandwidth using adaptive optimization of LSTM. Comput, Mater Cont. 2022;73(1):865–81.")], with recent advancements such as GraphCL-DTA leveraging graph contrastive learning to enhance representation quality in complex domains like drug-target interaction prediction [[10](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR10 "Yang X, Yang G, Chu J. GraphCL-DTA: a graph contrastive learning with molecular semantics for drug-target binding affinity prediction. IEEE J Biomed Health Inform. 2024;28(8):4544–52.")]. This is impactful in areas like consumer analytics. This article explores ML's significance in big data analytics. We present both empirical and experimental assessments of different methods. Through discussions, analyses, and evaluations, we give an in-depth understanding of ML techniques in big data. This guides researchers for better decisions. The article also discusses potential ML advancements in Big Data and future exploration avenues.

In this paper, we examine and assess algorithms that utilize ML to explore IoT, Social Media, NLP, and Information Security. In the "Big Data" age, extracting online insights has grown [[11](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR11 "Zhang D, Gong L, Ding S, Tian Y, Jia C, Liu D, Hu QN. FRCD: a comprehensive food risk component database with molecular scaffold, chemical diversity, toxicity, and biodegradability analysis. Food Chem. 2020;318:126470.")]. Emotions and opinions shared online shape netizen perceptions. Growing food safety concerns have spurred discussions on adulteration and agricultural pollution. These sentiment evaluations support risk communication and decisions [[12](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR12 "Bouzembrak Y, Steen B, Neslo R, Linge J, Mojtahed V, Marvin HJP. Development of food fraud media monitoring system based on text mining. Food Control. 2018;93:283–96.")]. The Internet is the primary source for food safety [[13](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR13 "Liu R, Pieniak Z, Verbeke W. Consumers’ attitudes and behaviour towards safe food in China: a review. Food Control. 2013;33(1):93–104.")], yet it propagates misinformation, intensifying concerns [[14](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR14 "Liu R, Pieniak Z, Verbeke W. Food-related hazards in China: consumers’ perceptions of risk and trust in information sources. Food Control. 2014;46:291–8.")].

Thus, analyzing online food safety sentiments is vital for risk control. In Big Data, real-time categorization and prediction of vast data are daunting due to fluctuating attributes [[15](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR15 "Nguyen H, Woon Y-K, Ng W-K. A survey on data stream clustering and classification. Knowl Inf Syst. 2015;45(3):535–69."),[16](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR16 "Zhang L, Lin J, Karim R. Sliding window-based fault detection from high-dimensional data streams. IEEE Trans Syst, Man Cybern Syst. 2017;47(2):289–303."),[17](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR17 "Tsimperidis I, Yoo P, Taha K, Mylonas A, Katos V. R2BN: an adaptive model for keystroke-dynamics-based educational level classification. IEEE Trans Cybern. 2020;50(2):525–35.")]. For instance, recent advances such as the AE-BiLSTM model with heuristic tuning proposed by Bhasha and Panda [[18](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR18 "Bhasha SJ, Panda S. Data privacy preservation and authentication scheme for secured IoMT communication using enhanced heuristic approach with deep learning. Cybern Syst. 2024. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/01969722.2024.2343982
                  
                .")] demonstrate robust privacy-preserving authentication for IoMT applications using deep learning in real-time streaming environments. Real-time tasks like recommendation engines [[19](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR19 "Gupta P, et al. Real-time Twitter recommendation: online motif detection in large dynamic graphs. Proc VLDB Endowment. 2014;7(13):1379–80."),[20](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR20 "Liu L, Xu J, Liao SS, Chen H. A real-time personalized route recommendation system for self-drive tourists based on vehicle to vehicle communication. Expert Syst Appl. 2014;41(7):3409–17."),[21](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR21 "Wang H, et al. R3: a real-time route recommendation system. Proc VLDB Endowment. 2014;7(13):1549–52.")] and spam filters [[22](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR22 "McCord M, Chuah M. Spam detection on Twitter using traditional classifiers,” in Proc. 8th Int. Conf. Auton. Trusted Comput, Banff, AB, Canada, 2011; pp. 175–186."), [23](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR23 "Jin X, Lin C, Luo J, Han J. A data mining-based spam detection system for social media networks. Proc VLDB Endowment. 2011;4(12):1458–61.")] necessitate such classification, focusing on efficiency. With digital data growing quickly, there's a push for advanced computational techniques [[24](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR24 "Elshabrawy M. A review on waste management techniques for sustainable energy production. Metaheur Optim Rev. 2025. 
                  https://doi.org/10.5421/MOR.030205
                  
                .")]. To address this, robust platforms are needed, and recent works such as Subramanian et al. [[25](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR25 "Subramanian N, Nikkath Bushra S, Shobana G, Radhika S. An optimal modified bidirectional generative adversarial network for security authentication in cloud Environment. Cybern Syst. 2024;55:1–33.")] propose advanced adversarial architectures like modified BiGAN-AH for securing cloud environments through AI-based authentication mechanisms. Big Data systems, delivers fast and scalable solutions marked by elasticity [[26](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR26 "Talia D. Toward cloud-based big-data analytics. IEEE Computer Science, 2013; pp. 98–101."),[27](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR27 "Agrawal D, Das S, El Abbadi A. Big data and cloud computing: current state and future opportunities. In Proceedings of the 14th international conference on extending database technology. ACM, 2011; pp. 530–533."),[28](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR28 "Taha K. Determining the semantic similarities among gene ontology terms. IEEE J Biomed Health Inform. 2013;17(3):512–25."),[29](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR29 "Taha K. Determining semantically related significant genes. IEEE/ACM Trans Comput Biol Bioinform. 2014;11(6):1119–30."),[30](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR30 "Alyasiri OM, Ali AH. Exploring GPT-4’s characteristics through the 5Vs of big data: a brief perspective. Babylonian J Artif Intell. 2023;2023:5–9."),[31](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR31 "Alyasiri OM, Akhtom D, Alrasheedy MN. An Overview of GPT-4’s characteristics through the lens of 10V's of big data. 2023 3rd International Conference on Intelligent Cybernetics Technology & Applications (ICICyTA), Denpasar, Bali, Indonesia, 2023; pp. 201–206")].

This survey offers a comprehensive review of Big Data Analytics in IoT, Social Media, NLP, and Information Security, using a consistent methodological framework to explore trends, challenges, and applications within these domains [[32](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR32 "Ikegwu AC, Nweke HF, Mkpojiogu E, et al. Recently emerging trends in big data analytic methods for modeling and combating climate change effects. Energy Inform. 2024;7:6.")]. A key contribution of this work is the introduction of a taxonomic framework that categorizes algorithms based on their specific applications, providing researchers with a clearer understanding of the relationships between techniques. The study integrates empirical evaluations, focusing on scalability, interpretability, accuracy, and efficiency, with experimental analyses that show the relative accuracy and computational time of various techniques within the same Big Data application domain.

By bridging empirical insights with experimental results, this paper not only highlights the strengths and weaknesses of various algorithms but also provides actionable guidance for selecting appropriate techniques. Ultimately, the survey lays the groundwork for future advancements in Big Data Analytics, offering researchers valuable tools and perspectives to address emerging challenges and drive innovation in this field.

Motivation and key contributions
--------------------------------

### Motivation

Despite the increasing number of survey papers in Big Data Analytics across domains such as IoT, Social Media, NLP, and Information Security, many fail to provide a structured and application-specific categorization of the machine learning techniques employed. Existing frameworks often adopt overly broad or domain-siloed classifications that overlook the nuanced, context-sensitive performance and applicability of different algorithms. This lack of granularity hinders meaningful comparisons, misguides algorithmic selection, and limits their practical utility.

To overcome these limitations, this paper introduces a novel taxonomic framework that systematically classifies machine learning algorithms based on their specific application domains and functional characteristics. Unlike prior categorizations that group algorithms under generic processing models or architectural layers, the proposed taxonomy integrates domain-specific roles, algorithmic techniques, and cross-domain mappings, thereby enabling a refined and context-aware analysis. It uniquely bridges theoretical classifications with empirical and experimental insights, thus offering a more operationally valuable lens for understanding and selecting appropriate techniques for Big Data tasks.

### Key contributions

Key contributions of this work include:

*   A novel, multi-domain taxonomic framework that categorizes algorithms not only by technique but also by their demonstrated suitability for specific tasks within IoT, Social Media, NLP, and Information Security. This framework emphasizes cross-domain applicability, highlighting analytical overlaps and reusable patterns—features absent from existing models.

*   A dual-mode evaluation approach that combines empirical benchmarking (on scalability, interpretability, accuracy, and efficiency) with experimental assessments of relative performance across domains. This duality enables precise algorithmic comparison within and across application contexts.

*   A comprehensive survey of trends, challenges, and real-world applications in Big Data Analytics, providing a holistic perspective while grounding the taxonomy in both academic and industrial relevance.

*   Practical guidance for algorithmic selection, empowering researchers and practitioners to make informed decisions based on domain-specific needs and performance trade-offs. For instance, the taxonomy identifies CNN as optimal for high-dimensional NLP and visual data, while XGBoost is preferred for structured and tabular IoT and security data.

In sum, this paper advances the state of the art by not only providing a broad review of Big Data Analytics across multiple domains but also introducing an actionable and distinctive methodological structure that enhances both academic understanding and practical deployment of machine learning algorithms in Big Data environments.

For instance, key findings of this study include the following insights:

*   Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are recommended for scenarios demanding the highest predictive accuracy and advanced structural modeling, particularly when handling graph-structured data in IoT applications.

*   GNNs are especially effective for tasks involving relational reasoning and influence detection, where deep representation of entity interactions is critical.

*   For mission-critical applications in information security—where precision, recall, and accuracy are paramount—either GNN or XGBoost can be deployed to ensure robust and dependable performance.

*   In natural language processing (NLP) domains that demand both high accuracy and scalability, XGBoost and Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) stand out as effective solutions due to their efficiency and adaptability to large-scale, unstructured data.

Having established a structured methodological taxonomy and its advantages over existing frameworks, the subsequent sections delve into its practical application. Each major domain—IoT, Social Media, NLP, and Information Security—is examined through this lens. For each domain, machine learning techniques are analyzed based on scalability, interpretability, accuracy, and efficiency. This structured comparative analysis allows us to assess the real-world applicability of the proposed taxonomy across various big data contexts.

Proposed methodological taxonomy and its advantages
---------------------------------------------------

### Proposed taxonomy

This section presents a novel taxonomy that classifies machine learning techniques in Big Data Analytics based on their functional roles across IoT, Social Media, NLP, and Information Security. Unlike traditional models, it offers a domain-specific, context-aware perspective and indicates where each technique is discussed in detail throughout the paper. Figure[1](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#Fig1) presents our taxonomy.

**Fig.1**

[![Image 3: figure 1](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs40537-025-01192-9/MediaObjects/40537_2025_1192_Fig1_HTML.png)](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9/figures/1)

A novel taxonomic framework that systematically classifies machine learning algorithms used in Big Data Analytics across key domains—IoT, Social Media, NLP, and Information Security—based on their specific application areas and functional characteristics. Unlike traditional categorizations that focus on generic models or architectural layers, this taxonomy integrates domain-specific roles and algorithmic techniques to provide a refined, context-aware, and practically valuable perspective. It also highlights the corresponding section numbers where each domain and technique is discussed in detail throughout the paper

[Full size image](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9/figures/1)

### Advantages of the proposed taxonomy

Advantages of the proposed taxonomy for Big Data Analytics

*   Holistic Integration:

    1.   o
Unifies multiple BDA domains: IoT, Social Media, NLP, and Information Security.

    2.   o
Moves beyond siloed approaches found in previous taxonomies.

*   Cross-Domain Insights:

    1.   o
Identifies overlapping patterns and analytical techniques.

    2.   o
Encourages reusability and robustness of methods across domains.

*   Practical Guidance:

    1.   o
Offers domain-specific algorithmic recommendations.

    2.   o
Provides actionable insights, not just theoretical classifications.

*   Technique Interconnection:

    1.   o
Emphasizes analytical method overlap and cross-applicability.

    2.   o
Fills a gap often overlooked in other taxonomies.

*   Distinctive Strengths Over Existing Frameworks:

    1.   o
_Scope & Comprehensiveness_: Covers a broad array of BDA areas.

    2.   o
_Domain Integration:_ Highlights cross-domain relationships.

    3.   o
_Real-World Utility:_ Geared for deployment, not just theory.

    4.   o
_Innovation:_ Introduces a novel, unified structure for BDA techniques.

### Real-world case studies where the proposed taxonomic framework has been applied

Our taxonomy has been implicitly validated through its application to numerous practical studies in IoT, Social Media, NLP, and Information Security. For instance:

*   _In IoT_, CNN-based and GAN-enhanced architectures have been deployed in smart transportation and security systems. Wu et al. [[33](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR33 "Wu H, Zhu Z, Zeng Z. Intelligent intrusion detection for internet of things security: a deep convolutional generative adversarial network-enabled approach. IEEE Access. 2021;9:123456–67.")] proposed a GAN-enhanced CNN model for intelligent intrusion detection at the edge, while Meng [[34](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR34 "Meng F. The convolutional neural network text classification algorithm in the information management of smart tourism based on internet of things. J Inf Sci. 2022;48(4):556–67.")] utilized CNN for sentiment analysis in smart tourism services. These implementations reinforce our taxonomy’s recommendation of CNNs for handling high-dimensional, unstructured data common in IoT environments.

*   _In Social Media_, deep learning and ensemble methods have shown effectiveness in sentiment and behavioral analysis. Xin et al. [[35](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR35 "Xin H, Wang Z, Li J. Depression detection via BERT-CNN and BERT-BiLSTM on reddit. Neurocomputing. 2022;475:108–21.")] used CNN model for depression detection on Reddit, and Anzum et al. [[36](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR36 "Anzum F, Banu A, Sultana R. Emotion detection from micro-blogs using novel input representation. Expert Syst Appl. 2022;193:116431.")] combined XGBoost with novel feature compression for emotion detection on Twitter. These case studies support our taxonomy’s emphasis on using hybrid and ensemble approaches for analyzing social media’s complex, multi-modal data.

*   _In NLP_, interpretable models such as decision trees have proven successful [[37](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR37 "Atteia G, Abdel Samee N, El-Kenawy ESM, Ibrahim A. CNN-hyperparameter optimization for diabetic maculopathy diagnosis in optical coherence tomography and fundus retinography. Mathematics. 2022;10(18):3274.")]. Miao & Song [[38](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR38 "Miao Y, Yu H, Song J. Medical big data statistical management system based on decision tree algorithm. 2022 IEEE 6th Advanced Information Technology, Electronic and Automation Control Conference (IAEAC), Beijing, China, 2022; pp. 2041-2046")] applied C4.5 decision trees for distributed medical big data management, while Narkhede et al. [[39](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR39 "Narkhede H, Khodke S, Patil S. Fake news detection system using decision tree algorithm and compare textual property with support vector machine algorithm. Conference: 2022 international conference on business analytics for technology and security (ICBATS). 2021.")] employed decision trees for fake news detection. These studies validate our taxonomy’s alignment of decision tree-based methods in NLP tasks.

*   _In Information Security_, robust algorithms such as XGBoost and CNN have been leveraged for threat detection [[40](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR40 "Gaber KS, Singla MK. Predictive analysis of groundwater resources using random forest regression. J Artif Intell Metaheur. 2025. 
                  https://doi.org/10.5421/JAIM.090102
                  
                .")]. Wang et al. [[41](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR41 "Wang J, Li B, Zeng Y. XGBoost-based android malware detection. 2017 13th International Conference on Computational Intelligence and Security (CIS). 2017; 268–272.")] developed a behavior-based XGBoost model for Android malware classification. CNN-based models like FSBDL have enabled real-time intrusion detection, as shown by Hussen et al. [[42](https://journalofbigdata.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40537-025-01192-9#ref-CR42 "Hussen N, Elghamrawy SM, Salem M, El-Desouky AI. A full

--- SOURCE 5: Social Media Research - Research Guides - University of Minnesota ---
URL: https://libguides.umn.edu/social-media-research

SUMMARY:
Social media: Data collection tools Tools for collecting data from social media platforms more broadly (not only from your own accounts). Social Feed ManagerOpen source software that harvests social media data and web resources from Twitter, Tumblr, Flickr, and Sina Weibo. Free social media data, analytics and visualization tools for researchers at all levels of expertise. Includes SMILE, an open-source social media analytics tool to collect and analyze data from Twitter and Reddit; and BAE, a Brand Analytics Environment to gain insight into how individuals and groups may interact with brands and various organizations. Social Media Data StewardshipLed by the Social Media Lab at Ryerson University, the project focuses on studying practices behind and attitudes towards the collection, storage, use, reuse, analysis, publishing and preservation of social media data.

FULL CONTENT:
Social Media - Social Media Research - Research Guides at University of Minnesota Minneapolis

===============
[](https://twin-cities.umn.edu/)[One Stop](https://onestop.umn.edu/)[MyU](https://www.myu.umn.edu/)[Give](https://www.lib.umn.edu/about/giving)[](https://www.lib.umn.edu/)[My Library Account](https://www.lib.umn.edu/myaccount)[Spaces](https://www.lib.umn.edu/spaces)[Services](https://www.lib.umn.edu/services)[Collections](https://www.lib.umn.edu/collections)[About](https://www.lib.umn.edu/about)[My Library Account](https://www.lib.umn.edu/myaccount)

Search

Social Media Research
=====================

*   [Social Media](https://libguides.umn.edu/c.php?g=855296&p=6430997)

Related guides
--------------

*   [Mass Communication](https://libguides.umn.edu/mass-communication) 
*   [News Sources](https://libguides.umn.edu/news-sources) 
*   [Professional Journalism](https://libguides.umn.edu/professional-journalism) 
*   [Social Media Research](https://libguides.umn.edu/social-media-research) 
*   [Strategic Communication](https://libguides.umn.edu/strategic-communication) 

Librarian
---------

[![Image 1: Profile Photo](https://d2jv02qf7xgjwx.cloudfront.net/apps/common/images/profile.jpg) Emma Molls](https://libguides.umn.edu/prf.php?id=5eab6834-7cdb-11ed-9922-0ad758b798c3)

A guide to tools and resources related to social media research and social media data sources.

Social listening & social media search
--------------------------------------

*   [Google Trends](https://trends.google.com/trends/explore)Compare search terms over time.  
*   [SOMAR: Social Media Archive at ICPSR](https://socialmediaarchive.org/record/52?ln=en)Researchers may apply for access to the Meta Content Library and API which incudes comprehensive access to the full public content archive from Facebook and Instagram. Offered in partnership with ICPSR.  
*   [TalkWalker](https://www.talkwalker.com/social-media-analytics-search)Free account for one project to run social media searches for brand tracking. Includes analytics with sentiment and simple demographic filters.  
*   [TweetDeck](https://tweetdeck.twitter.com/)Twitter’s official social media dashboard. Manage multiple Twitter accounts in a single dashboard, and create streams of feeds from people you follow, trending posts, or keyword searches of your choosing.  
*   [TweetReach (Union Metrics)](https://tweetreach.com/)Free snapshot reports based on user, hashtag and keywords. Requires permission to access you Twitter account. Subscription pro account also available.  

*   [Voxgov](https://libguides.umn.edu/19657)_This link opens in a new window_ This database of government documents also includes social media (e.g., Twitter feeds, YouTube videos) by government entities, including individual politicians. Requests for data downloads related to specific research projects are possible, contact Cody (chennesy@umn.edu) for more information.  

##### See also:

*   [ProQuest Congressional: Social Media Search](https://congressional.proquest.com/congressional/search/politicalnews/politicalnews)Find official social media posts and press releases from figures and departments of the U.S. government.  

Analytics for marketing
-----------------------

These tools allow marketers and others to analyze the reach and impact of _their own_ social media accounts.

*   [Facebook Audience Insights](https://www.facebook.com/business/news/audience-insights)A tool designed to help marketers learn more about their target audiences, including aggregate information about geography, demographics, and purchase behavior on Facebook.  
*   [Google Analytics](https://www.google.com/analytics/#?modal_active=none)Suite of web analytic tools for websites of all kinds.  
*   [HootSuite](https://hootsuite.com/)Manage social media campaigns and online content. [$]  
*   [Instagram Insights](https://www.facebook.com/help/788388387972460)Instagram Insights provide information on who your followers are, when they're online and more. See also: [Instagram for Business](https://business.instagram.com/).  
*   [Klear Influencer Marketing Tools](https://klear.com/free-tools)Use the free "Identify" tools on this page to search for social media influencers on various platforms.  
*   [Twitter Analytics](https://analytics.twitter.com/about)Measure engagement and learn how to make your own Tweets more successful.  
*   [YouTube Analytics](https://developers.google.com/youtube/analytics/)The YouTube Reporting and YouTube Analytics APIs let you retrieve YouTube Analytics data to automate complex reporting tasks, build custom dashboards, and much more.  

Social media: Data collection tools
-----------------------------------

Tools for collecting data from social media platforms more broadly (not only from your own accounts).

##### Start with:

*   [Twitter Academic Research](https://developer.twitter.com/en/solutions/academic-research)Apply for access to the Twitter historical archive through a new (as of Feb 2021) Academic Research API. Available to faculty, graduate students, and others who are affiliated with an academic institution and have a clearly defined research need. For questions about using the API reach out to dash@umn.edu.  
*   [Facepager](https://github.com/strohne/Facepager)Software for fetching public available data from Facebook, Twitter and other JSON-based APIs.  
*   [Machine Learning datasets (Hugging Face)](https://huggingface.co/datasets)The Hub enables researchers to access open user-contributed machine learning models and datasets.  
*   [Social Feed Manager](https://gwu-libraries.github.io/sfm-ui/)Open source software that harvests social media data and web resources from Twitter, Tumblr, Flickr, and Sina Weibo.  
*   [TAGS (Twitter Archiving Google Sheet)](https://tags.hawksey.info/)Use TAGS to setup and automate collection of tweets into Google Sheets. Collects data for the last 6-9 days (based on limitations of the Twitter API).  

##### See also:

*   [NCapture](https://chrome.google.com/webstore/detail/ncapture/lgomjifbpjfhpodjhihemafahhmegbek?hl=en)Chrome browser plug-in for getting Twitter and public Facebook data for use in NVivo.  
*   [netlytic](https://netlytic.org/)Netlytic is a community-supported text and social networks analyzer that can automatically summarize and visualize public online conversations on social media sites. It is made for researchers by researchers, no programming/API skills required.  
*   [Social Media Macroscope](https://socialmediamacroscope.org/)[Note: this resource is currently unavailable. LATIS is currently exploring whether they can provide a local instance of this tool for UMN researchers.] 

 Free social media data, analytics and visualization tools for researchers at all levels of expertise. Includes **SMILE**, an open-source social media analytics tool to collect and analyze data from Twitter and Reddit; and **BAE**, a Brand Analytics Environment to gain insight into how individuals and groups may interact with brands and various organizations.  
*   [Social Media Research Toolkit (Ryerson)](http://socialmediadata.org/social-media-research-toolkit/)A list of social media research tools curated by researchers at the Social Media Lab (Ryerson University). The kit features tools that have been used in peer-reviewed academic studies.  
*   [SocialMention](http://socialmention.com/)A social media search and analysis platform that aggregates user generated content into a single stream of information. Best suited for tracking brands and simple sentiment analysis.  
*   [Twitter (Overview of Tools)](http://blogs.lse.ac.uk/impactofsocialsciences/2017/05/08/using-twitter-as-a-data-source-an-overview-of-social-media-research-tools-updated-for-2017/)A brief overview of issues with Twitter data, as well as a directory of free tools for collecting data from Twitter.  

Analytics for research
----------------------

*   [ComScore Media Metrix](https://libguides.umn.edu/42176515)ComScore is an industry source for web analytics about major online properties, which is useful for consumer and audience research and for statistics and rankings of websites. more...less... University of Minnesota students and staff need to obtain individual accounts in order to use Comscore. Contact the [Social Sciences Librarian](https://www.lib.umn.edu/about/staff/molly-blake) to request an account.  

*   [Quantcast (website traffic estimates)](https://www.quantcast.com/top-sites)Use Quantcast's top sites and Measure tools to find traffic estimates for website domains (free account required).  

*   [Simmons Insights](https://libguides.umn.edu/18044)Survey data from the the Simmons National Consumer Survey to find target markets and consumer profiles for brands and products, including data on online platforms, social media and popular websites. Most recent 2 years not available. more...less... Most recent 2 years not available.  
*   [Statista](https://libguides.umn.edu/20259)_This link opens in a new window_ Statista provides statistical data on many topics including media, business, politics, society, technology and education. Sources include market reports, trade publications, scientific journals, and government databases. Statista's [Internet section](https://www.statista.com/markets/424/internet/) provides information on segments and industries such as social media, online search, user-generated content, online advertising and marketing as well as statistics on demographics and use.  
*   [Voxgov](https://libguides.umn.edu/19657)_This link opens in a new window_ Search publications, press releases, and social media activity of U.S. government agencies and legislative unites including legislators, department chiefs, and other branches in the federal government. Social media includes Twitter feeds, YouTube videos and other output by government entities. Congressional documents such as hearings are also available.  
*   [Winmo](https://libguides.umn.edu/50767066)Information about advertisers, agencies, public relations firms and media buying services. (formerly Advertising Redbooks)  

Social media & web datasets
---------------------------

*   [Awesome Public Datasets](https://github.com/awesomedata/awesome-public-datasets)A list of topic-centric high quality public data sources. Collected from blogs, answers, and user responses.  
*   [Blogger Corpus (2004)](http://u.cs.biu.ac.il/~koppel/BlogCorpus.htm)The collected posts of 19,320 bloggers gathered from blogger.com in August 2004. The corpus incorporates a total of 681,288 posts.  
*   [Common Crawl](http://commoncrawl.org/the-data/get-started/)The Common Crawl corpus contains petabytes of data collected from the web since 2008. It contains raw web page data, extracted metadata and text extractions.  
*   [Facebook Ad Categories (ProPublica)](https://www.propublica.org/datastore/dataset/facebook-ad-categories)This dataset includes two tables: data on the interest categories Facebook shows to users and the ad groups its shows to advertisers (2016).  
*   [Internet Archive (data)](https://blog.archive.org/2012/04/26/downloading-in-bulk-using-wget/)How to download files from [archive.org](https://archive.org/%22) in an automated way using wget.  
*   [Obama Administration Social Media Archives](https://obamawhitehouse.archives.gov/blog/2017/01/17/obama-administration-digital-transition-moving-forward)A directory of sites archiving various social media posts from members of the Obama Administration.  
*   [Political Ads from Facebook (ProPublica)](https://www.propublica.org/datastore/dataset/political-advertisements-from-facebook)This database, updated daily, contains ads that ran on Facebook and were submitted by thousands of ProPublica users from around the world (via browser extensions).  
*   [reddit APIs](https://www.reddit.com/dev/api/)Access data from posts, threads, comments, users and more from reddit and subreddits. Historical Reddit data has been collected at [http://files.pushshift.io/reddit/](http://files.pushshift.io/reddit/) as monthly CSV downloads.  
*   [Social Computing Data Repository (Arizona)](http://socialcomputing.asu.edu/)As a service to the Machine Learning, Data Mining, and Social Sciences communities, the Social Computing data repository currently hosts datasets from a collection of many different social media sites.  
*   [Social Media Archive (SOMAR at ICPSR)](https://socialmediaarchive.org/)A centralized repository of researcher social media data via ICPSR. Open to submissions of social media research datasets.  
*   [Stanford Large Network Dataset Collection (SNAP)](https://snap.stanford.edu/index.html)The SNAP library collects data on large social and information networks since 2004.  
*   [Tweet Datasets (DocNow)](https://www.docnow.io/catalog/)Directory of open-access tweet datasets on DocNow, available for research use. To convert Tweet IDs to JSON files of full-text tweets and metadata, use [DocNow's Hydrator app](https://github.com/DocNow/hydrator). See also: [UNLV's Twitter Data Tutorial Series](https://www.library.unlv.edu/whats_new_in_special_collections/2019/04/new-digital-collections-1-october-twitter-data-tutorial).  
*   [Twitter: Moral Foundations Corpus](https://osf.io/k5n7y/)35,108 Tweets curated from seven different domains of Twitter corpus that have been hand-annotated for 10 categories of moral sentiment.  
*   [Web Corpus (iWeb)](https://www.english-corpora.org/iweb/)14 billion words from 22 million web pages and 95k websites.  
*   [Wikipedia Data Dumps](https://dumps.wikimedia.org/)Monthly database backups of all Wikimedia wikis in various formats.  

#### See also:

*   [Finding data for your research (UMN)](https://www.lib.umn.edu/datamanagement/find)Check out the library's guide to subscription data archives and resources.  

Background sources
------------------

*   [![Image 2](https://syndetics.com/index.aspx?isbn=9781473916326/LC.GIF&client=springshare)SAGE Handbook of Social Media Research Methods (online)](http://dx.doi.org.ezp1.lib.umn.edu/10.4135/9781473983847)The SAGE Handbook of Social Media Research Methods offers a step-by-step guide to overcoming the challenges inherent in research projects that deal with "big and broad data," from the formulation of research questions through to the interpretation of findings.   
*   [![Image 3](https://syndetics.com/index.aspx?isbn=9781119183518/LC.GIF&client=springshare)Social Media Data Mining and Analytics (ebook)](https://onlinelibrary-wiley-com.ezp3.lib.umn.edu/doi/book/10.1002/9781119183518)Social Media Data Mining and Analytics shows analysts how to use sophisticated techniques to mine social media data, obtaining the information they need to generate amazing results for their businesses.   
*   [![Image 4](https://syndetics.com/index.aspx?isbn=9781107018853/LC.GIF&client=springshare)Social Media Mining](https://primo.lib.umn.edu/primo-explore/fulldisplay?docid=UMN_ALMA21605818630001701&context=L&vid=TWINCITIES&search_scope=default_scope&lang=en_US)Social Media Mining integrates social media, social network analysis, and data mining to provide a coherent platform to understand the basics and potentials of social media mining. It introduces the unique problems arising from social media data and presents fundamental concepts, emerging issues, and effective algorithms for network analysis and data mining.   

*   [Social Media Data Stewardship](http://socialmediadata.org/)Led by the Social Media Lab at Ryerson University, the project focuses on studying practices behind and attitudes towards the collection, storage, use, reuse, analysis, publishing and preservation of social media data.  
*   [Social Media Marketing: Video Tutorials (Lynda.com)](https://www.lynda.com/Social-Media-Marketing-training-tutorials/1366-0.html)Marketing courses and standalone videos on marketing for social media platforms such as LinkedIn, Facebook, Instagram, and Twitter.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Social media platforms like Twitter, Facebook, and Instagram provide rich data sources for sentiment analysis, user interactions, and trend tracking, enhancing social research methodologies.
IoT devices generate vast amounts of data through user interactions and environmental monitoring, offering insights into behavior patterns and real-time feedback for social research.
Administrative records include government databases and institutional datasets that provide structured information, aiding in demographic analysis and policy evaluation in social research.
